title: Probability Foundations
description: Counting, chance, conditioning and Bayes — the bedrock of all quant thinking.

levels.title: Counting the Ways
intro: Probability starts with counting. When outcomes are equally likely, P(event) = favorable outcomes / total outcomes.
Two workhorses: the multiplication rule (independent choices multiply: 2 coins → 2×2 outcomes) and permutations (n distinct items can be ordered n! ways).

exercises.prompt: A fair coin is flipped 3 times. How many equally likely outcome sequences are there?
exercises.explanation: Each flip has 2 outcomes and flips are independent, so 2 × 2 × 2 = 2³ = 8 sequences (HHH, HHT, HTH, …).

exercises.prompt: How many different ways can you arrange 4 distinct books on a shelf?
exercises.explanation: 4 choices for the first slot, 3 for the second, 2 for the third, 1 left: 4! = 4×3×2×1 = 24.

exercises.prompt: You draw the top card from a shuffled 52-card deck. What is the probability it's a heart?
exercises.explanation: 13 of the 52 cards are hearts: 13/52 = 1/4. Symmetry does the work — every suit is equally likely on top.

exercises.prompt: You roll two fair dice. How many of the 36 outcomes sum to exactly 7?
exercises.explanation: (1,6), (2,5), (3,4), (4,3), (5,2), (6,1) — six ordered pairs. 7 is the most common sum precisely because it has the most combinations.

exercises.prompt: So what's the probability two fair dice sum to 7?
exercises.explanation: 6 favorable outcomes out of 36 total: 6/36 = 1/6. Note how the previous count fed straight into this — counting IS probability here.

levels.title: Conditional Thinking
intro: New information changes probabilities. P(B|A) — the probability of B given A — is P(A and B) / P(A). You shrink your universe to the world where A happened.
Independence means information is useless: P(A and B) = P(A)·P(B). Drawing without replacement is the classic case where it fails.

exercises.prompt: P(A) = 0.5 and P(B) = 0.4, and A and B are independent. What is P(A and B)?
exercises.explanation: Independence lets you multiply: 0.5 × 0.4 = 0.2. If they weren't independent, you couldn't do this — you'd need P(B|A).

exercises.prompt: You draw 2 cards from a deck without replacement. Probability both are hearts?
exercises.explanation: P(1st heart) = 13/52, then only 12 hearts remain in 51 cards: (13/52)×(12/51) = 156/2652 = 1/17. The second draw is NOT independent of the first.

exercises.prompt: A family has two children. You learn at least one is a boy. What is the probability both are boys? (decimal, e.g. 0.25)
exercises.explanation: Equally likely families: BB, BG, GB, GG. 'At least one boy' removes GG, leaving 3 worlds; only BB has two boys → 1/3 ≈ 0.333. Conditioning shrank the sample space.

exercises.prompt: Monty Hall: you pick 1 of 3 doors, the host (who knows) opens a different door showing a goat and offers a switch. What should you do?
exercises.explanation: Your first pick wins 1/3 of the time. In the other 2/3, the host's forced reveal leaves the car behind the remaining door. Switching wins 2/3.

exercises.prompt: P(A and B) = 0.12 and P(A) = 0.3. What is P(B|A)? (decimal)
exercises.explanation: P(B|A) = P(A and B)/P(A) = 0.12/0.3 = 0.4. Given A happened, B happens 40% of the time.

levels.title: Bayes' Edge
intro: Bayes' rule tells you how to update beliefs with evidence: P(H|E) = P(E|H)·P(H) / P(E). The prior P(H) matters enormously — rare things stay fairly rare even after positive evidence.
Quant firms love these questions because markets are exactly this: updating a prior (fair value) on noisy evidence (order flow).

exercises.prompt: A disease affects 1% of people. A test catches 99% of sick people but false-positives on 5% of healthy people. You test positive. Probability you're sick? (decimal)
exercises.explanation: True positives: 0.01×0.99 = 0.0099. False positives: 0.99×0.05 = 0.0495. P(sick|+) = 0.0099/(0.0099+0.0495) ≈ 0.167. The tiny prior dominates — most positives are false.

exercises.prompt: Why was the answer above so much lower than 99%?
options: Base rate: healthy people vastly outnumber sick people, so their 5% false positives swamp the true positives
exercises.explanation: This is base rate neglect. 990 false positives per 100k people versus 990 true positives... the prior of 1% means evidence must be overwhelming to flip your belief.

exercises.prompt: Bowl A has 30 vanilla and 10 chocolate cookies; Bowl B has 20 of each. You pick a bowl at random and draw a vanilla cookie. Probability it was Bowl A? (decimal)
exercises.explanation: P(vanilla|A)=0.75, P(vanilla|B)=0.5, equal priors. P(A|vanilla) = 0.75/(0.75+0.5) = 0.6. Vanilla is evidence for A, but only mildly.

exercises.prompt: A bag holds one fair coin and one double-headed coin. You pick one at random, flip it, and see heads. Probability you hold the double-headed coin?
exercises.explanation: P(H|double)=1, P(H|fair)=1/2, equal priors: 1/(1+0.5) = 2/3. Heads is twice as likely from the trick coin, so it gets twice the posterior weight.

exercises.prompt: Same setup, but you flip the coin twice and see heads both times. Now what's the probability it's double-headed?
exercises.explanation: P(HH|double)=1 vs P(HH|fair)=1/4: posterior = 1/(1+1/4) = 4/5. Each new head multiplies the evidence — beliefs compound just like returns.
